coredata.dc:title: Photogeneration of reagent radicals for detection of ascorbic acid by using highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: An efficient and new hydrothermal method was established to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) in an autoclave, using citric acid and 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid as precursors. The characterization of N-CQDs was performed by using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The photoluminescence studies revealed that N-CQDs had a high quantum yield of 45.6 %. N-CQDs were used to develop a novel and exciting turn-off–on photoluminescence method for trace detection of ascorbic acid (AA). The photoluminescence of a mixture of N-CQDs and persulfate drastically decreased under UV irradiation in a flow injection setup. When AA was added to the mixture, the reagent radicals were scavenged. Therefore, the photoluminescence intensity was enhanced. Through the competitive mechanism, an efficient method was born to detect AA in some fruits, vegetables, and supplements. The sensing condition was optimized by a univariate procedure and a central composite design (CCD) approach. Under optimum conditions, the photoluminescence response was linear in the range of 0.3–11.5 µmol/L of AA. The AA sensing method exhibited a limit of detection of 0.05 µmol/L. It showed satisfactory sensitivity, repeatability, and accuracy. The AA assay method presented a sample throughput equal to 70 h−1.

head.ce:title: Photogeneration of reagent radicals for detection of ascorbic acid by using highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil 56199-11367, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil 56199-11367, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient and new hydrothermal method was established to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) in an autoclave, using citric acid and 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid as precursors. The characterization of N-CQDs was performed by using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The photoluminescence studies revealed that N-CQDs had a high quantum yield of 45.6 %. N-CQDs were used to develop a novel and exciting turn-off–on photoluminescence method for trace detection of ascorbic acid (AA). The photoluminescence of a mixture of N-CQDs and persulfate drastically decreased under UV irradiation in a flow injection setup. When AA was added to the mixture, the reagent radicals were scavenged. Therefore, the photoluminescence intensity was enhanced. Through the competitive mechanism, an efficient method was born to detect AA in some fruits, vegetables, and supplements. The sensing condition was optimized by a univariate procedure and a central composite design (CCD) approach. Under optimum conditions, the photoluminescence response was linear in the range of 0.3–11.5 µmol/L of AA. The AA sensing method exhibited a limit of detection of 0.05 µmol/L. It showed satisfactory sensitivity, repeatability, and accuracy. The AA assay method presented a sample throughput equal to 70 h−1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Various bottom-up procedures have been established to synthesize carbon-based quantum dots (CBQDs) [1–8]. In these methods, the thermalization of glucose [2,3], L-histidine [7], and citric acid (CA) [1,4–6,8] has been used as the main precursors. CA has been widely used to prepare CBQDs [1,4–6,8]. On the other hand, doping of N, S, and P has been employed as a powerful strategy to produce CBQDs. The synthesized CBQDs have different chemical, physical, and optical characteristics [1–4,6,8–16]. Nitrogen doping, for example, has been widely employed as an effective tool to increase the photoluminescence (PL) efficiency of CBQDs [1,2,4,6,8–10,12–15]. The doping strategy has been performed to prepare the appropriate CBQDs by using co-precursors such as ammonia [8], urea [1,4,15], L-cysteine [14], thiourea [6], and powdered sulfur [16] when CA was used as the main precursor. The prepared CBQDs have an appropriate structure with some oxygen-containing functional groups along the edges of their graphene layers to introduce the appropriate properties. In this way, different CBQDs have been available to react or interact with other chemicals to establish many applications in biological imaging [2,5,14,16] and chemical sensing [2,8,10,12,14]. The PL nature of CBQDs has been mainly used to develop the sensing methods [2,8,10,12,14,17–22], although some colorimetric approaches have also been reported [23,24]. Some recent PL-based sensing applications of CBQDs have been devoted to the detection of Cu(II) [12,17], Fe(II) [10], Fe(III) [2,10,14], Hg(II) [22], Cr(VI) [2,8], Levodopa [17], dopamine [18], Fe(II) and Fe(III) [19], L-cysteine [14], histamine [20], and caffeine [21]. The CBQDs have been doped with nitrogen [2,8,10,12,14,17,18,19,22] or sulfurous [14,20,21]. Also, in the analytical applications, the detection mechanisms were turn-off [2,8,10,12,18–20], turn-on [21], turn-off–on [14,17], and turn-on–off–on [22].

Ascorbic acid (AA), vitamin C, is a water-soluble vitamin and a biological antioxidant. AA is essential for the production of collagen in the body. It also helps the absorption of iron in plants [25]. The deficiency of AA in the body causes scurvy disease, symptoms such as muscle weakness, tooth loss, skin rash, fatigue, and joint pain [26]. This vitamin is naturally present in fruits and vegetables [27]. Detecting vitamin C in fruits, vegetables, biological samples, and supplements is biologically important.

Many photoluminescent quantum dots (QDs) [6,7,28–37] have been used to assay AA. Among them, AgInS2 [28], CuInZnS [29], and MoS2 [30] were inorganic-based QDs. The linear ranges for the AA detection methods were 0.6–99 [28], 0.05–0.25 [29], and 0.33–5 µmol/L [30]. Different sensing mechanisms were reported in these works [28–30]. AgInS2 QDs had some unsaturated metal sites (surface defects) to interact with AA (as a bidentate ligand), which enhanced the PL of AgInS2 QDs [28]. Also, the PL of thiochrome (produced due to the oxidation of thiamine by permanganate) was measured against the PL of CuInZnS QDs at 580 nm when AA was added to decrease the PL of the formed thiochrome at 459 nm [29]. In addition, the PL of MoS2 QDs-MnO2 nanocomposite enhanced when AA was added to the appropriate solution. AA reduced the MnO2 to decrease the inner filter effect (IFE) of MnO2 nanosheets. Therefore, the PL of MoS2 QDs decreased [30]. In addition to the inorganic QDs, some CBQDs have also been applied in AA sensing [6,7,31–37]. By using the organic QDs, AA was detected with a limit of detections equal to 1.2 [6], 0.06 [7], 0.0006 [31], 0.02 [32], 0.009 [33], 0.02 [34], 0.2 [35], 0.09 [36], and 0.04 [37] µmol/L. Different mechanisms have been defined for these AA detections [6,7,31–37]. Cr(VI) effectively quenched the PL of S, N-CQDs by the IFE mechanism. AA reduced Cr(VI) to increase the PL intensity of the S, N-CQDs [31]. In the other works, Fe(III) decreased the PL of CQDs [32,33] and N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) [34]. Fe(III) was reduced to Fe(II) by AA, and therefore, the PL intensity of the CBQDs increased [32–34]. The other work described that the silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were produced (from Ag(I) as the precursor) when AA was added. Then, AgNPs quenched the PL of CQDs via an energy transfer mechanism [35]. Also, two types of GQDs [6,36] were prepared and used to detect AA. Cu(II) quenched the PL of GQDs. When AA was added, Cu(II) was reduced to Cu(I), and therefore, the PL intensity of GQDs increased [6,36]. In addition, the complexation of AA (through its dienol hydroxyl groups) with NH2-CQDs caused the electron transfer from AA to NH2-CQDs, and therefore, the PL intensity of NH2-CQDs increased [7]. In another work, MnO2 nanosheets (in a composite with CQDs) were used for assaying AA by the IFE mechanism. AA reduced MnO2 to enhance the PL intensity of CQDs [37]. As can be seen, the mentioned PL-based methods are applicable for sensing AA in the range of 0.0006–1.2 µmol/L. In addition, the PL intensity increases by increasing the AA concentration in most of these batch methods due to the reducing properties of AA. The methods have not used the radical scavenging properties of AA, and flow injection setup in the AA detections. Flow injection methods provide the main advantages of fast analysis, low reagent consumption, and more safety.

Peroxides have exciting chemical properties because they have oxygen–oxygen bonds [38,39]. The most well-known peroxides are hydrogen peroxide and persulfate. Potassium persulfate is a well-known, cheap, and available peroxide. It is widely used in industries as bleach, additives in building materials, paints, food, and cosmetics [40]. Persulfate produces reactive radicals under UV irradiation or heat. Therefore, persulfate is used as a common initiator in radical polymerization. The UV light or heat breaks the O-O bond of persulfate to produce sulfate radicals (SO4 •-). SO4 •- produces hydroxyl radicals (•OH) in the aqueous solutions [38,39]. The lifetime of SO4 •- is in the range of 30–40 µs, but the lifetime of •OH is 0.02 µs. As a result, SO4 •- has a better chance than •OH to react with the appropriate inorganic or organic chemicals [41].

CBQDs have high PL intensity and water solubility. Therefore, they are often applied in cell imaging and PL-based sensing [2,5–8,10,12,14,16–22,31–37]. In addition, the unique structure (with appropriate functional groups) of CBQDs makes them radical scavengers [42–47]. The combination of high PL intensity and radical scavenging properties of CBQDs is promising to develop an exciting class of sensing methods. In this research, a new turn-off–on strategy is born for AA sensing in some fruits, vegetables, and supplements. At first, CA reacts hydrothermally with 3, 4-diaminobenzoic acid (DABA) to prepare new highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). Then, in a flow setup, the prepared N-CQDs react with the radicals produced from the UV irradiation of persulfate. Therefore, the PL intensity of N-CQDs decreases. The presence of AA in this mixture increases the PL intensity. Therefore, the competitive mechanism establishes a new sensing method.

Sodium hydroxide, CA, and AA were obtained from Merck (Germany). Hydrochloric acid, phosphoric acid, boric acid, acetic acid, and potassium persulfate were purchased from Loba-Chemie (India). DABA was purchased from Sigma (USA). The chemicals were of reagent or analytical grade. Deionized water was used in all experiments. A fresh solution of AA (stock) was prepared and used daily. Appropriate solutions of AA were prepared by successive dilutions with deoxygenated water.

One gram of CA and 0.1 g of DABA were dissolved in 10 mL of water. Then, the solution was placed in a 50 mL tightly closed Teflon autoclave (protected by a steel cylinder). The autoclave was heated in an oven at 200 °C for four hours. After that, the autoclave was transferred to ambient conditions, and the pH of the reaction mixture was adjusted to 7.0 (by adding sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric solutions). The solution was then dialyzed by a dialysis tube with a molecular weight cut-off equal to 2000 Da (Spectrum Lab). Dialysis of the solution was performed at ambient temperature and stirring for 24 h in a bath containing 1.0 L of deionized water (to remove the salts and low-molecular-weight chemicals). Then, the final volume of the dialyzed solution (bright yellow) was set to 50 mL using deionized water. The solution was stored at 4 °C. The solution was used as the stock solution. Appropriate dilute solutions were prepared using deionized water.

The flow injection setup was composed of a three-channel peristaltic pump (Minipuls 2, Gilson), a PEEK flangeless cross (VICI, USA), and a 6-port-2-position low-pressure injection valve (Valco, USA) to inject the samples. The silicon tubes for delivering persulfate and N-CQDs had an internal diameter of 0.8 mm. The samples were injected into the deionized water line (1.2 mm id). The sample volume was 100 μL. The PL of the resulting solution (as a time trace) was measured at 438 nm using an LS2-B spectrofluorimeter equipped with a 4 μL quartz flow-through cell. A broadband excitation filter (340 nm, FWHM 70 nm) excites the N-CQDs. A UV mini-reactor composed of a 6 W mercury lamp (Philips), a rolled Teflon tube (around the mercury lamp), and an aluminum foil (as the mirror) was also employed. The Teflon tube was used to form reagent radicals by UV irradiation. The internal diameter of the Teflon tube was 1.0 mm. Scheme 1 has described the UV irradiation-FI system.
The supplementary (section S1) has described the other instruments used in detail.

The FI setup in scheme 1 was used to analyze the samples at optimum chemical and instrumental conditions. In the FI setup, the PL of the mixture of N-CQDs and persulfate drastically decreased under UV irradiation. By injecting AA into the mixture (before UV irradiation), the reagent radicals (generated by UV irradiation) were scavenged. Therefore, the PL intensity was enhanced. The PL response was measured to calculate the AA concentration using a previously prepared calibration curve.

AA is present in various vegetables, fruits, and supplements. Some samples were tested to evaluate the applicability of the presented method. The sweet lemon and bell peppers (red, green, and yellow) were the analyzed food samples [48–51]. The supplementary (Section S2) has described the sample treatment in detail.

Fig. 1 A shows the UV–vis spectra of the solutions of CA, DABA, and the product (after hydrothermal reaction). As can be seen, the spectrum of the product in water is different from the reactants (CA and DABA). The first two peaks of N-CQDs at 220 and 253 nm are related to π → π* transitions. Furthermore, n → π* transitions correspond to the observed shoulder and the peak at 284 and 376 nm, respectively [52,53].

Various microscopic and spectroscopic techniques were used to characterize N-CQDs. The TEM image (Fig. 1B) revealed that N-CQDs were also relatively spherical. The image showed that the N-CQDs tend to be moderately aggregated due to surface interactions. Also, the TEM image showed that the size of the N-CQDs is 8.3 ± 2.9 nm (average size ± standard deviation). Therefore, the N-CQDs present a high degree of size non-uniformity. In addition, FESEM was used to present the surface morphology of the N-CQDs. The High-resolution FESEM image of N-CQDs (Fig. 1C) showed that N-CQDs were relatively spherical with moderate uniformity in size and shape. Also, the image presents an average size of about 10 nm for the N-CQDs. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was also employed to prepare the appropriate image from the synthesized N-CQDs. Fig. 1D shows the AFM image of N-CQDs mounted on a silicon wafer. The AFM studies also revealed a high degree of size non-uniformity for the N-CQDs. The size of the synthesized N-CQDs was 10.1 ± 5.6 nm (mean size ± standard deviation). The hydrodynamic size of N-CQDs was also measured by the DLS technique (Fig. 2 ). The mean diameter of the N-CQDs was 7.8 nm. The DLS study revealed that the N-CQDs are not uniform in size. Also, EDX analysis was used to characterize N-CQDs (Fig. 2). The analysis showed that N-CQDs contain carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen. The elemental mapping also showed that the distribution of the elements in N-CQDs is homogeneous. The elemental mapping clarified the presence of nitrogen as a minor element in N-CQDs.

XPS is a powerful tool for studying the chemical characteristics of N-CQDs. The XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs was obtained, and the peaks related to O, C, and N in N-CQDs were assigned (Fig. 3 A). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s is given in Fig. 3B. The peak at 530.7 eV is related to the oxygen of OH groups along the edges of N-CQDs. Also, the peak at 531.7 eV is attributed to the oxygen of the carbonyl groups and the carboxyl groups. Also, the oxygen of epoxy groups and the OH of carboxyl groups appeared at 533.0 eV [54–56].

The XPS spectrum of C 1s was deconvoluted to three peaks (Fig. 3C). The peak at 284.9 eV was attributed to C-C and CC. Also, the peak at 287.6 eV was related to carbon in CO, C-O, and CN groups. Furthermore, the peak at 288.8 eV was attributed to the carboxyl groups [12,57–60].

The XPS spectrum of N 1s was composed of three peaks (Fig. 3D). The peaks at 398.6 and 400.4 eV were attributed to the C-N-C of pyridinic groups and the nitrogen of the pyrrolic groups, respectively. The deconvoluted peak at 402.0 eV was related to the NH2 groups [58,60,61].

The FT-IR spectra of CA, DABA, and N-CQDs are given in Fig. 3E. It is observed that the spectrum of N-CQDs (the product) is entirely different from the precursors. N-CQDs show various absorption bands in the IR region. The broad absorption band at 3462 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the O-H band [12]. Also, the weak and broad absorption band at 3266 cm−1 and the triple absorption band in the range of 2820–3030 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H and the stretching vibration of the aliphatic C-H bands, respectively [56,62]. Furthermore, the broad and strong absorption band in the range of 1530–1750 cm−1 includes the C-N-H bending, CN stretching, aromatic C-C stretching, COO– asymmetric stretching, and CO stretching vibrations [12,23,56,62,63]. The bands at 1279 and 1396 cm−1 are attributed to the C-N stretching vibration and the symmetric stretching vibration of the COO– group, respectively [56,64]. Also, the C-O stretching vibration is related to the absorption band at 1155 cm−1 [56,64].

The XRD pattern and HRTEM images of N-CQDs have been exhibited in Fig. 4 . The XRD pattern (Fig. 4A) shows a broad diffraction peak centered at 25.1 owing to the highly disordered structure of N-CQDs, with the interlayer lattice spacing of the bulk graphite (006) plane of 0.37 nm surface structure [12,58]. Also, the HRTEM studies (Fig. 4B-C) show various lattice spacing for N-CQDs (0.18, 0.22, 0.25, 0.27, and 0.37 nm). The interlayer spacings are explained by assuming different layer structures in N-CQDs due to the doping of nitrogen and oxygen [12,58]. The lattice spacing equal to 0.18 nm is attributed to the facet of graphitic carbon (102). Furthermore, the spacings equal to 0.22, 0.25, and 0.27 nm can be attributed to the plane of graphite and, or lattice fringes of the graphene sheets (1120). Also, the lattice spacing of 0.37 nm is related to the interlayer lattice spacing of the planes of the bulk graphite (006).

The PL spectra of N-CQDs were recorded at different excitation wavelengths in the range of 260–400 nm with 20 nm intervals (Fig. 5 A). Fig. 5A showed the presence of a maximum emission wavelength at 438 nm (at the maximum excitation wavelength of 374 nm) for N-CQDs.

The PL quantum yield of N-CQDs (QYx) was also investigated. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol/L sulfuric acid (QYR = 0.54 %) was used as the reference (R) to obtain the QYx. Section 3 in the supplementary has discussed the study. The calculation showed that the QY of N-CQDs was equal to 45.6 % [65–68].

The PL spectra of N-CQDs (Fig. 5A) showed that the emission maxima were around 440 nm at different λex. Also, the shape of the PL spectra was not dependent on the excitation wavelength. This independence was attributed to the structural uniformity (and similarity in surface functional groups) of N-CQDs [69].

N-CQDs in aqueous solution were irradiated by the visible range of light (a 10 W LED) for different periods. Then, the PL intensity of N-CQDs was measured. Fig. 5B presents the results of the study. As was seen, the PL intensity of N-CQDs remained unchanged up to 40-minute irradiation period (the maximum tested period). The results showed excellent photostability of N-CQDs in the visible region of light. This property has introduced N-CQDs as a suitable PL probe for microscopy and spectroscopy. The effect of UV irradiation (6 W mercury lamp at 254 nm) was also investigated. Mercury low-pressure lamp emits UV light mainly at 185 and 254 nm. The energetic wavelengths produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) from oxygen (dissolved in water) and water. In addition, ROS are produced with different rate constants and activities. The results in Fig. 5B showed that the UV light had reduced the PL intensity of N-CQDs. The decrease is attributed to the reactions between the ROS radicals and N-CQDs. Also, as is observed, the photostability of the N-CQDs in the presence of 15 µmol/L ascorbic acid or p-aminophenol (as two typical radical scavengers) has increased. The effects of the radical scavengers to destroy the reactive radicals are considerable in the range of 0–10 min irradiation period. The property showed the suitability of N-CQDs for probing the radical scavengers. In the flow setup used, a Teflon tube (140 cm) with a 1.0 mm diameter was rolled around the mercury low-pressure lamp. For the 5.0 mL flow rate (optimum value), the calculations show an approximate residence time of 14 s for the solution (period of UV irradiation to the flowing solution). The period (14 sec) against the 2 min irradiation (batch experiment) is considerably lower. Therefore, the PL of N-CQDs does not considerably decrease under UV irradiation (without persulfate) in the flow setup.

Furthermore, the band gap for N-CQDs was calculated using the UV–vis spectrum. Fig. S1 and section 3 in the supplementary presented the details of the study. The study showed that the band gap was 2.12 ev [6,70–72].

In this work, the reactive reagent radicals were produced when UV light degraded persulfate. Then, the radicals reacted instantaneously with N-CQDs to decrease the PL intensity [38,39,73]. After sample injection, AA competed with N-CQDs to react with the reactive reagent radicals. Therefore, the PL of N-CQDs was enhanced. Scheme 2 revealed the detection mechanism for the AA assay.

As previously mentioned, FI-based methods provide various advantages over batch procedures [74]. Therefore, a FI manifold was designed to detect AA in this work. Scheme 1 shows details of the solutions and the assembled FI manifold. The affecting chemical parameters were persulfate, N-CQDs and sodium chloride concentrations, and pH. Also, the instrumental parameters were flow rate, the length of the UV reactor coil, the injected sample volume, and temperature. A univariate and a central composite design (CCD) method [75] were used to investigate the effects of the chemical parameters on the sensitivity of AA detection. The details of the studies are given in the supplementary (section 4, section 5, Tables S1–S3, and Figs. S2–S5).

The optimum condition proposed by CCD was (N-CQDs 5.8 µg mL−1, potassium persulfate 8.11 × 10-2 mmol/L, and pH = 5.02). Also, the univariate optimization method showed that the optimum condition was N-CQDs 5.2 µg mL−1, potassium persulfate 5.50 × 10-2 mmol/L, and pH = 6.80. As can be seen, the two optimization methods resulted in two different optimal conditions. However, only one of the optimal conditions must be applied in the subsequent studies. Hence, the two optimal conditions were compared. The optimal condition proposed by CCD was applied, and the PL intensity was measured. The PL intensity was 12.83 ± 0.62 (±amount is the standard deviation of four repetitive experiments). After that, the optimal condition obtained from the univariate optimization method was used, and the PL intensity was measured. The PL intensity was 12.12 ± 0.29 (four repetitive experiments). After that, the two series of results were compared with each other by using the paired t-test (P = 5 %). The amount of tcritical was 2.45, while the amount of texperimental was 2.05. Therefore, it is concluded that the two series of conditions did not cause different sensitivities. However, the condition proposed by CCD was selected for further experiments.

A time trace experiment was performed under the optimum conditions. Fig. 5C presents the effects of the parameters of UV light, persulfate plus UV light, and persulfate plus AA plus UV light on the PL intensity. As can be seen, six repetitive injections of an AA 7.7 μmol/L solution have produced reproducible peaks. This figure clearly explains the results of the test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Figures of merit and selectivity of the method
ce:section.ce:para: The critical analytical features of the method were investigated under optimal conditions. The calibration curve and its parameters have been given in Fig. 6 A and Table 1 , respectively. The linear calibration curve was in the range of 0.3–11.5 μmol/L.

A standard solution of AA equal to 0.15 µmol/L (the AA concentration was lower than the lowest determinable AA concentration) was prepared and then, analyzed seven times by the FI setup. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as follows: the standard deviation of the PL intensity measurements was calculated and multiplied by 3.14/m (m is the slope of the calibration curve) [76]. LOD was obtained equal to 0.05 μmol/L.

In addition, the accuracy, repeatability, and reproducibility of the method were evaluated. The relative standard deviation and recovery of the method for eight repetitive analyses of 2.83 μmol/L AA in one day were 2.3 % and 96.4 %, respectively. In addition, the method’s reproducibility on the analysis of 2.83 μmol/L AA was evaluated for seven days. The results of the studies have also been given in Table 1. As can be seen, the method has produced satisfactory results.

Different concentrations of some cations, anions, amino acids, organics, and phenolic compounds were tested to evaluate the selectivity of the method. The species were added individually to AA (5.7 µmol/L) with different mole ratios to prepare the appropriate binary mixtures. Fig. 6B shows the effects of the species on AA detection. The results showed that the most interfering species were phenolic compounds, arginine, Ni(II), Pb(II), Fe(III), and Mn(II).

An analytical method must be sufficiently robust and efficient. In this work, AA was determined in sweet lemon, different bell peppers, and some supplements. The results of the study are given in Table 2 . The sweet lemon and bell papers were analyzed by using the present method and the standard well-known methods [49,50]. The obtained recoveries and RSDs were 103.3–105.8 % and 2.2–4.7 %, respectively. As well, five different supplements were appropriately treated and analyzed. The detected AA values were compared with the nominal AA values in the supplements (Table 3). The recoveries and RSDs were 98.1–101.3 % and 1.4–4.8 %, respectively. The results of the method were compared with the nominal values of AA in the supplements by using a statistical t-test. All the obtained texperimental values were lower than the tcritical values at 95 % confidence level (tcritical is 3.18 for four repetitive experiments). As can be seen in Table 3 , analysis of the supplements of Maad and Hakim companies has resulted in statistically suitable results. Besides, blank control was performed for the supplements. The blank solutions were prepared and treated like the samples except that AA was absent. The Maad supplement contains 100 mg AA plus 27 mg Fe(II) (as sulfate), and the Hakim supplement contains 250 mg AA plus 2 mg saccharine. As shown in Fig. 6C, the Maad supplement presented a detectable small PL intensity (denoted as a in this figure). Also, two known amounts of AA (denoted as c and d) were added to the solution prepared from the supplement (denoted as b). The measured PL intensities revealed that the supplement matrix had no effects on the PL intensities of the solutions, and the PL intensities increased proportional to the amounts of the AA added. The results in Fig. 6C also revealed that the injection of the Hakim supplement blank (denoted as e) resulted in no PL signal. Besides, the PL intensity resulting from the solution (denoted as g) is proportional to the added AA. Furthermore, the obtained results of the present method were compared with the results of the standard method. The comparison exhibited that the values of texperimental were lower than the tcritical values for sweet lemon and bell peppers. As can be seen, there were no significant differences between the values obtained by the present method and the actual values (obtained by the standard method).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the AA assay method with other methods

ce:section.ce:para: Highly photoluminescent N-CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method. DABA and CA were dissolved in water and heated in an autoclave at 200 °C to synthesize N-CQDs. The properties and characteristics of the prepared N-CQDs were obtained by using various microscopic and spectroscopic techniques. N-CQDs showed an average size of 10 nm. They exhibited high PL (QY equal to 45.6 %) and high radical scavenging properties. N-CQDs reacted with the reagent radicals (formed by UV irradiation of persulfate), and their PL decreased. The reaction was used to develop a novel FI-based AA detection method. AA enhanced the PL of N-CQDs by deactivating the reactive radicals. The FI method was able to detect 0.05 μmol/L of AA. In addition, the method was used to determine AA in sweet lemon, various types of bell peppers, and supplements. The statistical t-test revealed no significant differences between the values obtained via the standard method and the results obtained via the present method. The method exhibited satisfactory repeatability, reproducibility, and accuracy. Also, the method was very fast on the sensing of AA (the sample throughput of the method was 70 h−1).

The results of the CCD study exhibited that the square of persulfate concentration, and the interaction between persulfate concentration and pH positively affected the PL intensity. Also, the square of pH, and the interaction between N-CQDs concentration and persulfate concentration had adverse effects on the PL intensity.

The basis of the method is applicable to develop some new UV irradiation-based sensing procedures for appropriate analysis. In addition, the fundamentals of the UV-based reagent radical formation method can be combined with N-CQDs or the other appropriate photoluminescent nanomaterials (as a detection setup) to detect appropriate analytes after reversed-phase high-performance liquid chromatography.
